definitions.definition: The person who writes the text, in this case, the playwright or writer of the interview.
The written or spoken content of the interview, including dialogue and narrative.
A conversation between two or more people, often recorded or written down, in which one person asks questions and the other person responds.
The story or sequence of events told in the text, including descriptions of people, places, and actions.
The spoken conversation between characters in the interview, including their words, tone, and body language.
A person or entity that appears in the text, including the interviewer, interviewee, and other individuals mentioned.
The person who asks questions in the interview, often seeking information or insight from the interviewee.
The person being interviewed, who responds to the interviewer's questions and shares their thoughts and experiences.
The time and place in which the interview takes place, including the location and any relevant details about the environment.
The attitude or mood conveyed by the text, including the emotions and feelings expressed by the characters.
The words, phrases, and expressions used in the text, including their connotations, idioms, and figurative language.
The way in which the reader is presented with information, including the perspective and point of view of the narrator.
The point of view or angle from which the story is told, including the character's thoughts, feelings, and experiences.
The position from which the story is narrated, including the character's thoughts, feelings, and experiences.
A narrator who provides information that is inaccurate, biased, or misleading, often due to their own emotions, experiences, or motivations.
A figure of speech in which the meaning is opposite of what is explicitly stated, often used to convey a particular tone or message.
A literary device that uses humor, irony, or irony to criticize or mock a particular idea, institution, or behavior.
The use of objects, colors, or other elements to represent abstract ideas or concepts, often carrying a deeper meaning or significance.
The use of language to create vivid sensory experiences, including visual, auditory, or other sensory details.
A reference to a person, place, event, or work of art, often used to create a particular mood or convey meaning beyond the literal text.
